content.full_text:
State of California A-78-176

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-5444

February 28, 1978


Assemblyman Gary Hart
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Assemblyman Hart:

Dan Lowenstein has asked me to respond to your
letter of January 12, 1978.

Your first question asks whether legislation should
be introduced to require Fair Board Directors and District Managers of District Agriculture Associations to
file Statements of Economic Interest.

According to our conflicts of interest staff, these
persons will be required to file conflicts statements
under the recently approved code of the Department of
Food and Agriculture. Under this code, which was
adopted by the Commission in January of this year, such
persons will be required to list:

(1) Investments in any business entity which,
during the reporting period, was awarded
or bid upon a contract of the Association
for, or supplied to it under contract,
materials, goods, supplies or services.

(2) Interests in real property which, during
the reporting period, was acquired by,
leased, or otherwise used by the Association for any consideration.

(3) Each source of income, provided the income
was furnished by or on behalf of any person,
furnishing or offering to provide real or
personal property or services to the
Association as stated in paragraphs (1)
and (2) above or any gift from any such
person, including concessionaires.

Assemblyman Gary Hart
Page Two                                    February 28, 1978


     These officials' first report will be filed this
spring.

     You have also asked how much may a person contribute
anonymously. Under current law, no person may make or
receive an anonymous contribution of $50 or more. (Government Code Section 84304) We are sponsoring legislation
which will be carried by Assemblyman Bill Thomas to
raise this threshold as well as the reporting threshold
to $100.

     Please feel free to call on me if you have further
questions about the matters raised in your letter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 445-8925

DISTRICT OFFICE
1123-B STATE ROUTE 5
SANTA BARBARA
CALIFORNIA 93102
(805) 687-1755

MERRY SEABERG
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

Assembly
California Legislature

COMMITTEES
EDUCATION
HEALTH
WAYS AND MEANS
COMMISSION ON THE
STATUS OF WOMEN

GARY K. HART
ASSEMBLYMAN, THIRTY-FIFTH DISTRICT

CHAIRMAN
SUBCOMMITTEE ON EDUCATIONAL REFORM

January 12, 1978

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Dan:

My Assembly District includes the 19th and 37th
District Agricultural Associations. On several occasions,
Fair Board Directors and other persons in my district
have expressed the concern that there is a potential for
conflict of interest on the part of the Directors and
District Managers.

It is my understanding that Directors and Managers
establish contracts for building maintenance and construction,
trophies, and exclusive food catering service that often
run into thousands of dollars. While these contracts are
reviewed by the Department of Food and Agriculture,
Division of Fairs and Expositions, these contracts are
often with small, local firms and the financial interests
of Directors and Managers is not usually known.

Two years ago my staff discussed this with Carlotta
Mellon's office and found they do have a form that is
sometimes given to potential Fair Board appointees, but
this is not always done. It seems that legislation to
require conflict of interest statements would be appropriate.

I would be interested in your opinion regarding
such conflict of interest requirements for Fair Board
Directors and Managers, and whether or not you feel
legislation would be an appropriate response.

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Page 2
January 12, 1978

     I do not know what the chances of legislative success
might be given the general lack of enthusiastic support
for other conflict of interest requirements on the part
of my colleagues, but I think it should be seriously
considered. Legislation might be difficult to pass,
but I would be willing to try if there is a need.

     On another matter, I have been asked by two legis-
lative committee staff members what is the maximum anonymous
contributions they can make to an upcoming legislative
campaign. It's unfortunate staff members feel they need
to protect their anonymity, but I think this is probably
the only way they can contribute without possible profes-
sional reprisals later on. Could you bring me up to
date on the provisions of the law in this regard?

                                        Sincerely,


                                        GARY K. HART

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Page 2
January 12, 1978


I do not know what the chances of legislative success

sional reprisals later on. Could you bring me up to

Sincerely,

GARY K. HART

sections.question: s about the matters raised in your letter.
sections.conclusion: According to our conflicts of interest staff, these persons will be required to file Conflicts statements under the recently approved code of the Department of Food and Agriculture. Under this code, which was adopted by the Commission in January of this year, such persons will be required to list investments in business entities with the Association, interests in real property used by the Association, and each source of income from persons furnishing property or services to the Association. These officials' first report will be filed this spring. Under current law, no person may make or receive an anonymous contribution of $50 or more. We are sponsoring legislation which will be carried by Assemblyman Mo Thomas to raise this threshold as well as the reporting threshold to $100.
sections.question_synthetic: Should legislation require Fair Board Directors and District Managers to file economic interest statements, and what are the limits on anonymous campaign contributions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Fair Board Directors and Managers are already required to file economic interest statements under a newly approved Department of Food and Agriculture code effective this year. Regarding anonymous contributions, current law prohibits anonymous contributions of $50 or more, and the FPPC is sponsoring legislation to raise this threshold to $100.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains OCR artifacts and formatting issues. Date discrepancy: document ID indicates 1978, but response letter is dated February 1979 responding to January 1978 request. Multiple versions of the incoming letter appear in the document. The FPPC response clearly addresses both questions posed.

These officials' first report will be filed this spring. Under current law, no person may make or receive an anonymous contribution of $50 or more. We are sponsoring legislation which will be carried by Assemblyman Mo Thomas to raise this threshold as well as the reporting threshold to $100.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Robert M. Stern responds to Assemblyman Gary Hart's inquiry about whether legislation is needed to require Fair Board Directors and District Managers to file economic interest statements. The FPPC advises that such filing requirements have already been implemented through a newly adopted Department of Food and Agriculture code, and addresses Hart's second question regarding limits on anonymous campaign contributions under current law.
